text: What does it mean to be present to another person? Nagel's framework suggests something demanding: to treat someone else as a subject, not merely an object, requires imaginative entry into their first-person perspective. Martin Buber called this the I-Thou relation — a meeting of subjects — as opposed to I-It, in which the other is treated instrumentally. Genuine attention to another person is not data collection; it is allowing their subjectivity to affect yours. Distracted listening, by contrast, reduces the other to a source of auditory input while the mind pursues its own associations.
question: In Buber's framework, what distinguishes the I-Thou relation from the I-It relation?

options.text: I-Thou involves emotional attachment, while I-It is a neutral form of perception
I-Thou is a meeting of subjects, while I-It treats the other instrumentally
I-It describes deep listening, while I-Thou describes distracted interaction
I-Thou refers to interaction with objects, while I-It refers to interaction with persons
